questions.subject: ap-comparative-government
questions.question: According to the table, which of the following best describes the relationship between regime category and Freedom House rating?

choices.text: All democratic regimes receive a 'Free' rating, while all authoritarian regimes receive a 'Not Free' rating.
choices.explanation: The table shows Mexico and Nigeria as democratic but rated 'Partly Free,' contradicting the claim that all democratic regimes receive 'Free' ratings.

choices.text: Democratic regimes tend to score higher on civil liberties than authoritarian or hybrid regimes, though outcomes vary within each category.
choices.explanation: The UK (Free) anchors the democratic category, while Mexico and Nigeria (Partly Free) score higher than the authoritarian China/Iran and hybrid Russia (Not Free), showing a tendency without a perfect correspondence.

choices.text: The Freedom House rating and regime category measure the same phenomenon and always produce identical classifications.
choices.explanation: Freedom House ratings and regime category are correlated but not identical — Mexico and Nigeria are classified as democratic yet receive only 'Partly Free,' illustrating that these are distinct measures.

choices.text: Hybrid regimes receive the same Freedom House rating as authoritarian regimes, proving that the two regime types are functionally equivalent.
choices.explanation: Although Russia (hybrid) and Iran/China (authoritarian) all receive 'Not Free' ratings in this table, a shared rating does not make regimes functionally equivalent — their internal structures, mechanisms of control, and legal frameworks differ significantly.

questions.subject: ap-comparative-government
questions.question: Which of the following best distinguishes a regime from a government?

choices.text: A regime names the specific elected officials currently holding office, while a government names the underlying constitutional and normative rules that determine who may hold power and for how long.
choices.explanation: This reverses the correct definitions: governments consist of specific officeholders, while regimes are the durable underlying rules and norms governing access to power.

choices.text: A regime is the durable ruleset for accessing power; a government is the current officeholders who exercise it.
choices.explanation: Per PAU-1.A.2, regimes are durable frameworks (lasting across leadership changes), while governments are the current officeholders who change through elections, coups, or succession.

choices.text: A regime and a government are interchangeable terms that both refer to the group of people currently holding political power.
choices.explanation: Regime and government are distinct: one regime can have many successive governments; confusing them is a common misconception the CED specifically addresses.

choices.text: A regime refers exclusively to authoritarian systems, while a government refers to democratic systems.
choices.explanation: Both democratic and authoritarian political systems have regimes and governments; the terms are analytical categories that apply across all regime types, not labels for specific types.

questions.subject: ap-comparative-government

stimulus.content: "Mexico's 31 states and the Federal District each have their own constitutions, governors, and legislatures that independently manage education policy and local law enforcement. In contrast, China's provincial governments implement policies handed down from the central government in Beijing; provincial leaders are appointed by the central party apparatus and may be transferred or removed at will."

questions.question: The passage best illustrates which of the following distinctions?

choices.text: The difference between a parliamentary system and a presidential system.
choices.explanation: Parliamentary vs. presidential distinctions concern executive-legislative relations, not the distribution of power between central and subnational governments — which is what federalism vs. unitary systems address.

choices.text: The fundamental structural difference between a federal and a unitary system
choices.explanation: Mexico exemplifies a federal system in which subnational governments have constitutionally defined authority, while China exemplifies a unitary system in which subnational governments exist at the central government's discretion and operate under central direction.

choices.text: The difference between a democratic regime and an authoritarian regime.
choices.explanation: While Mexico is democratic and China is authoritarian, the passage's specific focus is on the vertical distribution of power (federal vs. unitary), not on the democratic vs. authoritarian character of each regime.

choices.text: The difference between centralization and decentralization within unitary states.
choices.explanation: Mexico is not a unitary state — it is a federal state; describing the Mexico–China contrast as a difference within unitary states misidentifies Mexico's structure.

questions.subject: ap-comparative-government

stimulus.content: "The Islamic Republic of Iran operates according to the principle of velayat-e faqih — the governance of the Islamic jurist. Under this doctrine, supreme political authority rests with a senior Shia cleric who serves as Supreme Leader for life. The Supreme Leader commands the armed forces, appoints the head of the judiciary, oversees candidate vetting through the Guardian Council, and sets the overall direction of state policy. Elected institutions including the president and parliament operate within boundaries set by unelected clerical bodies."

questions.question: The passage best illustrates which source of power and authority?

choices.text: Rational-legal authority derived from a written constitution
choices.explanation: Rational-legal authority is grounded in formal laws and constitutions; the passage emphasizes religious doctrine (velayat-e faqih) as the basis for supreme authority, not constitutional procedure.

choices.text: Traditional authority based on hereditary succession
choices.explanation: Traditional authority relies on long-standing custom and dynastic succession; the passage describes authority grounded in Islamic jurisprudence and religious qualification, not hereditary lineage.

choices.text: Religious ideology as the primary source of legitimacy
choices.explanation: The passage explicitly grounds Iran's supreme authority in the Shia Islamic doctrine of velayat-e faqih, matching PAU-1.D.1b, which identifies the 1979 transition to theocracy based on Islamic Sharia law as Iran's defining source of power.

choices.text: Charismatic authority derived from a leader's personal popularity
choices.explanation: Charismatic authority depends on a leader's personal appeal; the passage describes an institutionalized religious doctrine (velayat-e faqih) that transcends any individual, making it theological rather than personal in character.

questions.subject: ap-comparative-government

stimulus.content: "Our movement seeks to overthrow not merely one president but the entire political system that has oppressed the people under the Shah. We do not merely want to change who rules; we want to change the rules themselves — the basis on which power is held, the institutions through which it is exercised, and the ideology that justifies it." (paraphrased from 1979 Iranian revolutionary discourse)

questions.question: The speaker's argument best reflects which of the following political science concepts?

choices.text: Government change through elections
choices.explanation: Electoral government change replaces officeholders while leaving the fundamental rules of the regime intact; the speaker explicitly demands changing 'the rules themselves,' which goes beyond electoral turnover.

choices.text: Regime change through revolution
choices.explanation: The speaker calls for replacing the fundamental rules, institutions, and ideology — not just the current leadership — which matches the definition of regime change through revolution (PAU-1.D.3, PAU-1.D.4).

choices.text: Democratic consolidation
choices.explanation: Democratic consolidation refers to a regime maturing and stabilizing after a democratic transition; this passage describes the beginning of an anti-regime revolutionary movement, not institutional maturation.

choices.text: Devolution of power to regional governments
choices.explanation: Devolution involves transferring specific powers from a central to regional governments while the regime structure remains intact; the speaker demands total systemic change, not decentralization.

questions.subject: ap-comparative-government

rows: States have own constitutions and governors
89 subjects; central government has reasserted power
Devolution to Scotland, Wales, Northern Ireland
Provinces have no independent legislative authority

questions.question: Which of the following conclusions is best supported by the data in the table?

choices.text: Federal systems guarantee stronger protections for ethnic minority rights than unitary systems.
choices.explanation: The table shows structural arrangements, not outcomes for minority rights; federalism in Nigeria has allowed Sharia law enforcement in some states that some minorities oppose, demonstrating that the relationship between federalism and minority rights is not automatic.

choices.text: The United Kingdom is a federal state because it has devolved powers to Scotland and Wales.
choices.explanation: The table correctly classifies the UK as unitary; devolution is a policy choice from the center, not a constitutional division of sovereignty, and Westminster retains parliamentary supremacy.

choices.text: Both federal and unitary systems can shift in their degree of centralization over time.
choices.explanation: Russia (federal, but reasserting central power) and the UK (unitary, but with devolution) both illustrate that the federal/unitary label does not fix the actual degree of centralization in practice, consistent with PAU-2.A.2.

choices.text: Unitary states are always more politically stable than federal states.
choices.explanation: The table provides no data on political stability; Iran and China are unitary but face significant internal pressures, while the UK is unitary and highly stable — the table does not support a direct correlation between system type and stability.

questions.subject: ap-comparative-government

stimulus.content: A political scientist notes: "Although Country X holds regular elections, opposition parties face significant barriers to registration, state-controlled media dominates political coverage, and electoral thresholds have been raised to prevent smaller parties from gaining legislative seats. The incumbent party has won every election since the system's founding."

questions.question: Country X is best described as which of the following regime types?

choices.text: Liberal democracy
choices.explanation: Liberal democracies feature free and fair elections, independent media, and rule of law; Country X's barriers to registration, state media control, and threshold manipulation contradict all three criteria.

choices.text: Hybrid or managed democracy
choices.explanation: The passage describes the hallmarks of a managed democracy: elections exist but are manipulated through registration barriers, media control, and threshold rules to ensure a predetermined outcome — the defining characteristics of Russia's political system (PAU-1.B.3, PAU-1.D.1d).

choices.text: Theocratic state
choices.explanation: A theocracy grounds authority in religious doctrine and clerical rule; the passage describes electoral manipulation by a political elite, not governance by religious institutions or law.

choices.text: One-party authoritarian state
choices.explanation: A one-party state legally prohibits other parties; Country X allows opposition parties to register, though it erects barriers to their success — this resembles a managed democracy more than a formal one-party state like China.

questions.subject: ap-comparative-government

stimulus.content: "The process of democratization does not end with the first competitive election. A regime becomes consolidated when democratic institutions are so broadly accepted and deeply embedded that no major political actor would gain by attempting to overturn them. At this stage, democracy has become 'the only game in town.'"

questions.question: Which of the following course countries best exemplifies the condition described in the passage?

choices.text: Russia, which holds regular elections and has a multiparty legislature
choices.explanation: Russia's elections are systematically manipulated and the regime suppresses opposition — far from democratic consolidation; calling Russia consolidated contradicts the passage's criterion that no actor gains by overturning democracy.

choices.text: Mexico, which transitioned from PRI dominance to competitive multiparty elections in 2000
choices.explanation: Mexico's 2000 transition was a significant democratization step, but with ongoing corruption concerns and incomplete institutional consolidation, Mexico is better described as a transitional or partly-consolidated democracy, not fully consolidated.

choices.text: United Kingdom, whose parliamentary system and rule of law are deeply embedded
choices.explanation: The UK's centuries-old parliamentary tradition, strong rule of law, and deeply embedded democratic institutions match the description of a consolidated democracy where no major actor contests the system's legitimacy (PAU-1.C.5).

choices.text: Nigeria, which returned to civilian rule in 1999 and has held successive competitive elections
choices.explanation: Nigeria has made important democratic progress, but governance challenges, electoral violence, and institutional fragility indicate democratic consolidation is still incomplete — successful elections alone do not constitute consolidation.

questions.subject: ap-comparative-government

stimulus.content: "A nation is a group of people with a shared identity — language, ethnicity, history, or political aspiration. A state is a political-legal entity with a permanent population, governing institutions, and international recognition over a defined territory. The two need not coincide."

questions.question: Which of the following best applies the distinction described in the passage to a course country?

choices.text: Nigeria is a nation because its citizens share a single ethnic identity.
choices.explanation: Nigeria contains over 250 distinct ethnic groups and does not share a single ethnic identity; it is a multinational state, not an ethnic nation in the passage's sense.

choices.text: The Kurdish people constitute a nation without a state, while Nigeria is a state containing many nations.
choices.explanation: This correctly applies the nation/state distinction: Kurds share a common cultural identity but lack their own state, while Nigeria is a single state housing over 250 distinct ethnic nations — precisely the 'not coinciding' scenario the passage describes.

choices.text: China is a nation-state because the Han majority aligns its national identity perfectly with the state's borders.
choices.explanation: China contains 55 recognized ethnic minorities including Uighurs and Tibetans whose national identities differ from the Han; China is a multinational state, not a pure nation-state where cultural and political boundaries are perfectly aligned.

choices.text: Iran is a nation because its citizens are unified under Islamic law.
choices.explanation: Iran has significant ethnic diversity (Persians, Azerbaijanis, Kurds, Baluchis) and religious divisions (Shi'a majority, Sunni minority, Christians, Jews); shared religion does not erase these national distinctions, and Iran is a state, not simply a unified nation.

questions.subject: ap-comparative-government

stimulus.content: "In 2000, Vicente Fox of the National Action Party (PAN) won the Mexican presidential election, defeating the candidate of the Institutional Revolutionary Party (PRI) for the first time in 71 years. The transition was peaceful, with the outgoing PRI government accepting and ratifying the results."

questions.question: The event described in the passage is most significant as an example of which of the following?

choices.text: A revolution that replaced the existing regime with a new political system
choices.explanation: A revolution involves the forcible replacement of the regime's fundamental rules; Mexico's 2000 election was a peaceful transfer of power within existing institutions, not a systemic overthrow.

choices.text: Democratization via peaceful power transfer after decades of dominant-party rule
choices.explanation: The 2000 Mexican election exemplifies PAU-1.C.1 and PAU-1.D.1c: the transition away from PRI one-party dominance through a competitive election demonstrates democratization via peaceful power transfer — a landmark for Mexico's trajectory.

choices.text: Democratic consolidation, since Mexico's democracy was fully institutionalized by 2000
choices.explanation: The 2000 election was a major step in Mexico's democratization process, not the endpoint of consolidation; significant governance and corruption challenges remained, and full consolidation requires much deeper institutional embedding.

choices.text: A coup d'état in which the military transferred power to a new leadership
choices.explanation: The passage explicitly states this was an electoral victory and peaceful transition; a coup involves illegal, forcible seizure of power — the opposite of what occurred in Mexico in 2000.

questions.subject: ap-comparative-government
questions.question: A student argues: 'The data in the table proves that higher GDP per capita causes countries to become democracies.' Which of the following statements best evaluates this claim?

choices.text: The claim is valid because the UK has both the highest GDP and a 'Free' rating.
choices.explanation: A single confirming case does not prove causation — especially when China's and Russia's similar GDP figures but 'Not Free' ratings contradict the causal claim.

choices.text: The table establishes a statistical correlation at best; the causal direction remains unsupported.
choices.explanation: Per MPA-1.A.3 and MPA-1.A.4, correlation (an association between variables) does not establish causation, and comparative politics has numerous confounding variables; China and Russia have similar GDP per capita but remain 'Not Free,' undermining any causal claim.

choices.text: The claim is disproven because Nigeria and Mexico have lower GDP per capita than Russia yet receive identical or similar Freedom House freedom ratings, which contradicts the predicted direction of the relationship.
choices.explanation: This argument shows an anomaly in the correlation but conflates disproving the causal claim (which is valid) with the methodological principle about correlation vs. causation — the more fundamental point is that the table cannot establish causation regardless.

choices.text: The claim is accurate because the Freedom House rating and GDP per capita measure the same underlying variable.
choices.explanation: Freedom House rates political rights and civil liberties; GDP measures economic output — they are distinct measures of different phenomena and do not measure the same underlying variable.

questions.subject: ap-comparative-government

stimulus.content: "Following a disputed election, mass protests erupted across the country's major cities. Millions demanded that the true results be published and that the declared winner step down. Security forces deployed to disperse protesters, and hundreds of activists were arrested. International observers expressed concern, but the regime declared the official result final."

questions.question: The events described in the passage are most consistent with which of the following learning objectives about political legitimacy?

choices.text: Election integrity disputes can weaken legitimacy and ongoing democratization processes.
choices.explanation: Per LEG-1.B.5, questions about election integrity across course countries can lead to protests that undermine legitimacy; the passage precisely illustrates this dynamic — matching Iran's 2009 Green Movement context.

choices.text: Peaceful transfer of power reinforces legitimacy regardless of protest activity.
choices.explanation: The passage shows the opposite: the regime rejected the peaceful transfer of power by dismissing protests and arresting activists, which undermined rather than reinforced legitimacy.

choices.text: Economic development is the primary source of legitimacy for all regime types.
choices.explanation: The passage deals with electoral integrity and protest, not economic performance; while economic development is one source of legitimacy (LEG-1.A.2), the passage focuses on contested elections as a legitimacy threat.

choices.text: Devolution of power to regional governments strengthens legitimacy by addressing citizen grievances.
choices.explanation: The passage describes a centralized regime suppressing protests over election results — not a devolution of power to subnational governments; devolution is irrelevant to the scenario described.

questions.subject: ap-comparative-government

stimulus.content: "China's leaders draw political legitimacy from multiple sources simultaneously: the Communist Party claims a historical mandate derived from its revolutionary victory and its role in ending foreign humiliation; it also invokes its record of delivering extraordinary economic growth and poverty reduction; and it justifies rule through an ideology of socialism with Chinese characteristics."

questions.question: Which of the following best explains why China's regime relies on multiple simultaneous sources of legitimacy rather than elections alone?

choices.text: China holds competitive multiparty elections at both local and national levels, making electoral legitimacy an available but insufficient source of authority on its own.
choices.explanation: China does not hold competitive multiparty elections; the CPC controls all governmental and electoral mechanisms, so electoral legitimacy in the democratic sense is simply unavailable as a primary source.

choices.text: Authoritarian regimes cannot draw legitimacy from any source other than military coercion.
choices.explanation: Per LEG-1.A.2, both democratic and authoritarian regimes can derive legitimacy from nationalism, ideology, economic performance, and tradition; reducing authoritarian legitimacy solely to coercion is a common misconception.

choices.text: Without free elections, the CPC builds popular acceptance via nationalism and economic gains.
choices.explanation: LEG-1.A.2 specifically identifies nationalism, ideology, and economic performance as alternative sources of legitimacy; China's use of all three simultaneously reflects its need to build popular acceptance without the electoral mandate available to democratic governments.

choices.text: China uses multiple sources of legitimacy because its constitution requires it to do so.
choices.explanation: China's regime relies on multiple legitimacy sources out of political necessity, not constitutional requirement; the relationship between constitutional provisions and actual legitimacy-building behavior is not one of legal mandate.

questions.subject: ap-comparative-government

stimulus.content: "The Movement for the Emancipation of the Niger Delta (MEND) emerged from communities that had received little economic benefit from decades of oil extraction in their region, despite living amid the pipelines and refineries. By attacking oil infrastructure and taking foreign workers hostage, MEND raised the cost of continued extraction and drew international attention to the distribution of oil revenues."

questions.question: MEND's activities best illustrate which of the following concepts from the course?

choices.text: Civil society monitoring of government malfeasance through legal advocacy
choices.explanation: MEND used militant tactics including attacks on infrastructure and hostage-taking — not legal civil society advocacy; it is an armed internal actor, not a conventional civil society organization.

choices.text: A non-state actor challenging state authority through organized armed conflict
choices.explanation: Per LEG-1.C.1, internal actors can undermine regime stability and challenge state authority; MEND exemplifies how an armed group emerging from unresolved economic grievances can threaten both physical security and the regime's legitimacy.

choices.text: A separatist movement seeking full international recognition of the Niger Delta as a legally sovereign independent state separate from the Nigerian federation
choices.explanation: MEND sought equitable distribution of oil revenues and better living conditions for Niger Delta communities — not the creation of an independent state; its demand is resource equity, not secession.

choices.text: A democratization movement aimed at achieving multiparty elections in Nigeria
choices.explanation: MEND's grievances were economic (oil revenue distribution), not about expanding electoral competition; its goals were distinct from the broader democratization process that Nigeria underwent through institutional electoral reforms.

questions.subject: ap-comparative-government

stimulus.content: "Scholars debate whether Nigeria's return to civilian rule in 1999 represents genuine democratization or merely a transfer of power from one group of elites to another. Electoral competition has intensified since 2015, when the All Progressives Congress defeated the incumbent People's Democratic Party — the first democratic alternation of power at the national level. Yet governance challenges, persistent electoral violence, and weak institutional independence temper optimism about the depth of democratic consolidation."

questions.question: Which of the following arguments does the passage most directly support?

choices.text: Nigeria has achieved full democratic consolidation because it has held successive competitive elections since 1999.
choices.explanation: The passage explicitly qualifies Nigeria's democratic progress with 'governance challenges, persistent electoral violence, and weak institutional independence' — these conditions contradict the claim of full consolidation.

choices.text: Nigeria has experienced democratic backsliding comparable to Russia's managed democracy.
choices.explanation: The passage identifies Nigeria's challenges but does not equate them with Russia's systematic engineering of managed democracy; Nigeria has genuine alternation of power (2015), which Russia has not experienced.

choices.text: Democratic progress and incomplete consolidation can coexist within the same transitioning polity.
choices.explanation: The passage precisely illustrates PAU-1.C.1 and PAU-1.C.5 in tension: Nigeria's 2015 power transfer represents meaningful democratic progress, yet incomplete institutionalization means full consolidation remains unachieved — democratization as process, not endpoint.

choices.text: Nigeria's 2015 APC victory conclusively proves that first-past-the-post electoral systems invariably produce full democratic consolidation once a single genuine alternation of power occurs at the national level.
choices.explanation: The passage attributes Nigeria's progress to competitive political conditions and institutional reform, not to the electoral system specifically; moreover, the passage explicitly acknowledges ongoing governance challenges that prevent full consolidation.

questions.subject: ap-comparative-government

stimulus.content: "Russia's political system retains the formal architecture of liberal democracy — a president elected by popular vote, a bicameral parliament, a constitution, and registered political parties. Yet analysts classify Russia as an illiberal democracy or competitive authoritarian regime because elections are held under conditions that systematically advantage the ruling party, opposition candidates face registration barriers and selective prosecution, and independent media have been largely suppressed."

questions.question: The passage best illustrates which of the following arguments about the relationship between elections and democracy?

choices.text: The presence of formal democratic institutions is sufficient to classify a country as a liberal democracy.
choices.explanation: The passage directly challenges this view: Russia maintains formal democratic institutions but is classified as illiberal precisely because those institutions do not function competitively or independently.

choices.text: Electoral quality and competitiveness, not mere occurrence, determine a regime's democratic character.
choices.explanation: Per PAU-1.B.1c (electoral integrity) and PAU-1.B.3, holding elections is not sufficient; what matters is whether they are free and fair, the media is independent, and opposition can compete — Russia's elections fail these tests, making it an illiberal democracy.

choices.text: Russia cannot be classified as authoritarian because it holds elections and allows multiple parties to register.
choices.explanation: The passage classifies Russia as a competitive authoritarian regime despite holding elections; the CED explicitly recognizes hybrid regimes that combine formal democratic structures with authoritarian manipulation.

choices.text: Countries that have adopted written constitutions have achieved democratic consolidation.
choices.explanation: Russia has a written constitution but is classified as illiberal; a constitution's existence does not guarantee democratic consolidation — enforcement, independence of institutions, and genuine competition are what matter.

questions.subject: ap-comparative-government

stimulus.content: "After decades of PRI dominance, Mexico undertook a series of institutional reforms in the 1990s. The government established an independent National Electoral Institute (INE) to reduce fraud, implemented gender quotas for party candidate lists, and eliminated the practice of el dedazo — the informal custom by which the outgoing president hand-picked his successor."

questions.question: Which of the following best explains how the reforms described in the passage relate to the process of democratization?

choices.text: The reforms reduced political corruption by transferring the state's economic functions to the private sector.
choices.explanation: The passage describes electoral and institutional reforms (INE, gender quotas, eliminating el dedazo), not economic privatization; privatization is a separate dimension of Mexico's reform process, not the one described here.

choices.text: The reforms boosted electoral integrity, female representation, and open competition — all goals of democratization.
choices.explanation: Per PAU-1.C.1 and PAU-1.C.3, democratization aims for greater fairness and transparency in elections, increased citizen participation, and protection of civil rights; INE independence reduces fraud, gender quotas increase representation, and eliminating el dedazo removes elite control over candidate selection.

choices.text: The reforms created a managed democracy by concentrating power in the INE rather than in the PRI.
choices.explanation: An independent electoral commission is the opposite of managed democracy; INE was created precisely to prevent the PRI from managing outcomes, making elections more genuinely competitive rather than less.

choices.text: Mexico's reforms demonstrated that regime change must occur through revolution rather than incremental institutional adjustment.
choices.explanation: Mexico's democratization occurred through incremental institutional reforms within the existing regime, not through revolution; this is precisely an example of PAU-1.D.3's concept of incremental change producing regime transformation over time.

questions.subject: ap-comparative-government

stimulus.content: "The degree to which power is centralized or decentralized can change over time in both federal and unitary states. Russia, constitutionally a federation, underwent significant recentralization in the early 2000s: gubernatorial elections were abolished and replaced with presidential appointments, and federal district envoys were created to oversee regional compliance with central directives."

questions.question: Which of the following best explains the significance of the Russian recentralization described in the passage?

choices.text: It transformed Russia from a federal system to a unitary system by constitutional amendment.
choices.explanation: Russia did not amend its constitution to become a unitary state; recentralization occurred through policy changes (appointing governors, creating federal districts) while Russia's formal federal structure remained in place.

choices.text: Centralization in a federal system can shift substantially without changing the formal system label.
choices.explanation: Per PAU-2.A.2, the degree of centralization can change over time even within the same system type; Russia remained formally federal while centralizing substantially, demonstrating that system labels do not fix power distribution in practice.

choices.text: It shows that the Russian federal system was functioning correctly by devolving power to the regions.
choices.explanation: The passage describes the opposite: recentralization reduced regional autonomy by replacing elected governors with presidential appointees — this is centralization, not devolution.

choices.text: Russia's recentralization measures strengthened its democratic governance by ensuring that federal policy goals were implemented uniformly and accountably across all eighty-plus constituent regions.
choices.explanation: Abolishing gubernatorial elections is a democratic setback, not an advance; removing elected offices reduces political accountability and citizen influence over regional governance — the opposite of democratic strengthening.

questions.subject: ap-comparative-government
questions.question: Which of the following correctly classifies all six AP Comparative Government course countries by their territorial organization?

choices.text: Federal: Mexico, Nigeria, and Russia; Unitary: China, Iran, United Kingdom.
choices.explanation: Per PAU-2.A.1, Mexico, Nigeria, and Russia are the CED's three federal examples; China, Iran, and the UK are the three unitary examples — this is the canonical classification students must know.

choices.text: Federal: Mexico, Nigeria, United Kingdom; Unitary: China, Iran, Russia
choices.explanation: The UK is a unitary state despite its devolution of powers; Russia is a federal state despite its recentralization — this classification incorrectly switches the UK and Russia.

choices.text: Federal: China, Russia, Iran; Unitary: Mexico, Nigeria, United Kingdom
choices.explanation: China and Iran are unitary states, not federal; Mexico and Nigeria are federal states — this classification is the inverse of the correct CED answer.

choices.text: Federal: Mexico, Russia; Unitary: China, Iran; Mixed: Nigeria, United Kingdom
choices.explanation: The CED does not classify any course country as 'mixed'; Nigeria is formally federal, and the UK is formally unitary — introducing a mixed category misrepresents the canonical CED distinctions.

questions.subject: ap-comparative-government

stimulus.content: "Across the six course countries, governments employ various strategies to maintain legitimacy. Some rely primarily on economic performance — delivering growth, reducing poverty, or improving living standards. Others invoke religious heritage or nationalist ideology. Still others ground their authority in a history of revolutionary struggle or the promise of social equality."

questions.question: Which of the following pairs of course countries and their primary legitimacy sources is most accurate?

choices.text: China: economic performance and CPC ideology; Iran: religious authority (velayat-e faqih)
choices.explanation: Per LEG-1.A.2, China's CPC draws legitimacy from its economic growth record and communist ideology, while Iran grounds authority in the Shia Islamic doctrine of velayat-e faqih — both are explicitly named in the CED as primary legitimacy sources for these countries.

choices.text: Russia: rule of law and constitutional authority; Nigeria: military power and coercion
choices.explanation: Russia's legitimacy under managed democracy relies more on nationalist ideology, economic performance (oil era), and strong presidential authority than on rule of law; Nigeria's current civilian government draws legitimacy from electoral competition, not military coercion.

choices.text: Mexico: PRI party endorsement as primary legitimacy source; UK: economic growth above all else
choices.explanation: PRI dominance has ended in Mexico — party endorsement is no longer the primary legitimacy source; the UK grounds legitimacy principally in rule of law, constitutional tradition, and democratic elections, not solely economic growth.

choices.text: Nigeria: religious authority; UK: one-party ideological mandate
choices.explanation: Nigeria's federal constitution maintains secular governance at the national level; the UK is a multiparty democracy, not a one-party state — both descriptions are fundamentally inaccurate for their assigned countries.

questions.subject: ap-comparative-government

stimulus.content: "The United Kingdom's Parliament passed legislation granting Scotland its own Parliament with authority over education, health, and some taxation. The Scottish Parliament cannot, however, alter constitutional matters, defense policy, or foreign affairs — all of which remain with Westminster. Parliament at Westminster theoretically retains the power to repeal or amend the devolution statutes."

questions.question: Which of the following best explains why the United Kingdom remains classified as a unitary state despite the Scottish devolution described in the passage?

choices.text: Westminster created Scotland's Parliament by statute and can repeal it, so sovereignty remains with the central government.
choices.explanation: Unitary states concentrate supreme authority at the center; devolution is a policy grant, not a constitutional division of sovereignty — Westminster's retained power to repeal devolution statutes confirms the UK's unitary character (PAU-2.A.1).

choices.text: Scotland cannot be considered a subnational unit because it has its own Parliament and taxation authority, making it functionally sovereign.
choices.explanation: Having a Parliament and some taxing authority does not make Scotland a co-sovereign tier; even in federal systems subnational units have legislatures and tax powers — what matters is whether those powers are constitutionally entrenched against central removal.

choices.text: The UK is unitary because it lacks a written constitution.
choices.explanation: The absence of a single codified constitution is a feature of the UK's constitutional system, but federal vs. unitary classification depends on the distribution of sovereign authority, not the presence or absence of a written document.

choices.text: Devolution proves the UK is a federal state because subnational governments exercise real powers.
choices.explanation: Exercising real powers is not the same as holding constitutionally entrenched powers; in federal systems, subnational authority is constitutionally protected against unilateral central removal — Scotland's powers lack that protection.

questions.subject: ap-comparative-government
questions.question: Which of the following best defines sovereignty as the term is used in comparative politics?

choices.text: The degree to which a government is popular with its citizens
choices.explanation: Popularity relates to legitimacy, not sovereignty; a deeply unpopular government still possesses sovereignty as long as it maintains supreme legal authority over its territory.

choices.text: A state's supreme legal authority over its own territory and population
choices.explanation: PAU-1.A.4 defines sovereignty as the right and power to govern without outside interference; it is the defining attribute of a state as a political-legal entity.

choices.text: The formal constitutional document that defines and grants power to elected government officials
choices.explanation: A constitution is one mechanism for organizing governmental power, but sovereignty refers to the supreme authority itself, not to the document through which authority is organized.

choices.text: The democratic legitimacy that any government must earn by successfully conducting free, fair, and internationally recognized competitive elections on a regular basis
choices.explanation: Sovereignty is distinct from democratic legitimacy; authoritarian states possess sovereignty without democratic elections — the two concepts are analytically separate.

questions.subject: ap-comparative-government

stimulus.content: "The Communist Party of China has exercised uninterrupted governing control since 1949. Its authority rests on its monopoly over military command through the People's Liberation Army, its control of the state apparatus, and its ideological claim to represent the interests of the Chinese people. The party's constitution places it above all other state institutions."

questions.question: The passage best illustrates which of the following sources of power and authority in China?

choices.text: Traditional authority based on dynastic succession
choices.explanation: Traditional authority relies on hereditary succession and historical custom; the CPC's authority derives from its revolutionary history, military control, and ideological claim — not dynastic lineage.

choices.text: CPC control over the military and state as the foundation of regime stability
choices.explanation: Per PAU-1.D.1a, the CPC's control over China's military provided the power and authority to maintain regime stability; the passage precisely describes how party supremacy over the military, state, and ideology sustains CPC governance.

choices.text: Rational-legal authority derived from a constitution popularly ratified by Chinese citizens
choices.explanation: China's constitution formally subordinates all institutions to the CPC, and the constitution is not ratified by popular vote; this arrangement is better described as rule by law (using legal instruments for party control) than as rational-legal authority constraining the party.

choices.text: Charismatic authority centered on a single universally beloved national leader
choices.explanation: The passage emphasizes institutional sources of CPC power (military control, state apparatus, ideology), not the personal charisma of an individual leader — though individual leaders may supplement this with personal authority.

questions.subject: ap-comparative-government

stimulus.content: "Nigeria's multiparty republic emerged from decades of military rule. From independence in 1960 through 1999, the country experienced multiple coups and military governments before returning to civilian rule. The 1999 constitution established a federal presidential system with competitive elections, a bicameral legislature, and an independent judiciary."

questions.question: Nigeria's political history as described in the passage is best characterized as an example of which of the following?

choices.text: Democratic consolidation, since Nigeria now has a functioning constitution
choices.explanation: A functioning constitution is a necessary but not sufficient condition for democratic consolidation; the passage describes a transition process, not a completed consolidation — and Nigeria's ongoing governance challenges reinforce that consolidation remains incomplete.

choices.text: Democratization following military rule, with the 1999 constitution as a milestone
choices.explanation: PAU-1.C.1 and PAU-1.D.1c both address Nigeria's transition from military rule to a multiparty republic as an instance of democratization; the 1999 constitution represents a major milestone in this ongoing process.

choices.text: A coup d'état that forcibly installed the current civilian government outside constitutional norms
choices.explanation: Coups install governments by force; Nigeria's 1999 transition was a negotiated return to civilian rule through elections and constitutional drafting, not a forcible seizure of power by civilians.

choices.text: Gradual liberalizing reform within an otherwise stable and continuous authoritarian one-party civilian state apparatus that remained in control throughout the independence period
choices.explanation: Nigeria's history involved military regimes, not a one-party civilian state; the transition to competitive multiparty elections represents a more fundamental change than incremental reform within a stable authoritarian party system.

questions.subject: ap-comparative-government
questions.question: A researcher argues: 'Countries with lower Gini scores (more equal income distribution) will always have higher Freedom House ratings.' Which row in the table provides the strongest evidence against this claim?

choices.text: Nigeria, with a Gini equal to the UK's but a lower freedom rating
choices.explanation: Nigeria and the UK have identical Gini scores (~35) but very different Freedom House ratings (Partly Free vs. Free); this directly contradicts a claim of an invariant relationship between income equality and political freedom.

choices.text: Mexico, which has both the highest Gini inequality score in the table and a correspondingly low 'Partly Free' Freedom House rating
choices.explanation: Mexico's high Gini and Partly Free rating is consistent with the researcher's direction (less equal → less free), so it actually supports rather than challenges the claim.

choices.text: China, which has both a high Gini score and a 'Not Free' rating
choices.explanation: China's high inequality and Not Free rating is consistent with the researcher's argument (higher inequality → lower freedom); it does not provide counterevidence.

choices.text: Russia, which has a moderate Gini index and a 'Not Free' rating
choices.explanation: Russia's moderate Gini and Not Free rating could fit the researcher's pattern (moderate inequality → moderate freedom restriction); it is a less sharp counterexample than Nigeria's identical Gini to the UK with a much lower freedom rating.

questions.subject: ap-comparative-government

stimulus.content: "A political scientist writes: 'Many authoritarian states hold regular elections, and voter turnout in those elections is often very high — sometimes surpassing rates in established democracies. Yet these elections do not make the regimes democratic.'"

questions.question: Which of the following best explains the argument the political scientist is making?

choices.text: High voter turnout is the primary indicator that an election is fair and competitive.
choices.explanation: This contradicts the political scientist's argument: high turnout can result from mobilization, coercion, or manufactured participation, not genuine voluntary engagement in competitive elections.

choices.text: Elections qualify as genuinely democratic only when they result in an actual alternation of governing power at every single electoral contest held.
choices.explanation: Even democratic elections can be won repeatedly by the same party; what matters is that genuine competition exists and power could transfer — not that it must transfer in every election.

choices.text: Simply holding elections does not make a regime democratic if civil liberties are absent.
choices.explanation: Per PAU-1.B.1c, electoral integrity (not mere occurrence) determines democratic character; authoritarian regimes use elections to manufacture legitimacy and collect 'illusions of influence,' without offering genuine competition.

choices.text: Authoritarian regimes cannot hold elections because their constitutions prohibit it.
choices.explanation: Authoritarian regimes regularly hold elections; the passage's entire premise is that they do hold elections — prohibiting them would contradict the argument the political scientist is making.

questions.subject: ap-comparative-government

stimulus.content: "In the months following the 2011 State Duma elections in Russia, thousands of citizens gathered in Moscow and other cities to protest what they alleged were fraudulent results. Demonstrators held signs reading 'Russia Without Putin' and demanded new elections under fair conditions. The protests represented the largest wave of civic mobilization in Russia since the 1990s."

questions.question: The 2011 Russian protests described in the passage are most directly an example of which of the following?

choices.text: Internal actors who challenge a regime's legitimacy through mass protests and civic unrest
choices.explanation: Per LEG-1.B.5 and LEG-1.C.3, citizen protest groups can challenge legitimacy when election integrity is questioned; the 2011 protests exemplify this — Russian citizens acting as internal actors contesting the regime's electoral legitimacy.

choices.text: A successful example of democratic consolidation in Russia following disputed elections
choices.explanation: Democratic consolidation would mean democratic institutions are broadly accepted and stable; the protests revealed severe legitimacy deficits and were followed by intensified repression — the opposite of consolidation.

choices.text: An example of devolution strengthening regional political participation across Russia
choices.explanation: Devolution refers to transferring powers to subnational governments; the protests were a direct civic challenge to the central regime's electoral integrity, not related to devolution.

choices.text: A separatist movement demanding Russian regional autonomy from the central government
choices.explanation: The protesters demanded fair national elections and political reform — not regional independence or autonomy; this was a national legitimacy challenge, not a separatist movement.

questions.subject: ap-comparative-government

stimulus.content: "Political scientists distinguish between empirical and normative statements. 'Russia's Freedom House rating declined from Partly Free to Not Free between 2004 and 2006' is one type of statement. 'Russia should restore democratic rights to its citizens' is another type."

questions.question: Which of the following correctly identifies the type of each statement in the passage?

choices.text: Both statements are empirical because they describe actual political conditions in Russia.
choices.explanation: The second statement ('should restore') is a normative claim about what ought to happen — it cannot be verified empirically; only the first statement about a measurable rating change is empirical.

choices.text: The first statement is empirical (factual/objective); the second statement is normative (a value judgment about what should be).
choices.explanation: Per MPA-1.A.5, empirical statements are factual and testable (Freedom House ratings can be observed and verified); normative statements express value judgments about what ought to be ('should restore') and cannot be proven true or false by evidence.

choices.text: The first statement is normative because it judges Russia's political conditions; the second is empirical because it prescribes a specific policy.
choices.explanation: This inverts the definitions: describing a measurable rating change is empirical; prescribing what 'should' happen is normative — the passage's first statement reports a fact, the second expresses a value.

choices.text: Both statements are normative because they both relate to Russia's political system.
choices.explanation: Relating to a political system does not make a statement normative; the first statement is objectively verifiable (empirical), while the second expresses a value preference (normative) — topic alone does not determine the type.

questions.subject: ap-comparative-government

stimulus.content: "The Zapatista Army of National Liberation launched an armed uprising in Chiapas on January 1, 1994 — the same day NAFTA took effect. The rebels, drawn largely from indigenous communities, declared that NAFTA would destroy their livelihoods by exposing Mexican agriculture to cheap North American imports. Rather than seeking to overthrow Mexico's national government, the Zapatistas demanded land rights, autonomy for indigenous communities, and democratic reforms."

questions.question: Which of the following best characterizes the Zapatista uprising's relationship to regime stability in Mexico?

choices.text: The uprising was a revolutionary movement that successfully replaced Mexico's national regime with a new political system.
choices.explanation: The Zapatistas did not seek to overthrow the national regime; they demanded autonomy and democratic reforms within the existing state — their movement is an internal actor challenge, not a successful revolution.

choices.text: The Zapatistas were an internal actor using armed pressure and political demands to push Mexico toward democratic reform.
choices.explanation: Per LEG-1.C.1b, state responses to separatist group violence in Mexico are a named CED example; the Zapatistas exemplify an internal actor leveraging armed pressure and indigenous rights demands to influence state policy — not systemic regime change.

choices.text: The Zapatistas were an external actor because their grievances were tied to NAFTA, an international trade agreement.
choices.explanation: The Zapatistas were a domestic indigenous movement in Chiapas — they are unambiguously an internal actor; the fact that their grievances related to NAFTA does not make them an external force.

choices.text: The uprising strengthened Mexico's path toward democratic consolidation by demonstrating that the civilian regime was capable of tolerating and negotiating with armed internal opposition groups rather than simply annihilating them.
choices.explanation: Democratic consolidation is not measured by tolerance of armed opposition; the Zapatista conflict revealed governance failures and legitimacy deficits related to inequality and indigenous marginalization — it complicated rather than straightforwardly advanced consolidation.

questions.subject: ap-comparative-government

stimulus.content: "The Transparency International Corruption Perceptions Index ranks countries annually based on perceived levels of public sector corruption, with a score of 100 indicating highly clean government and a score near 0 indicating highly corrupt government. Higher-income countries with strong rule-of-law traditions tend to cluster near the top; countries dependent on extractive industries often score lower."

questions.question: How should political scientists most appropriately use the Corruption Perceptions Index (CPI) as a data source?

choices.text: As a definitive, objective measure of actual corruption that can be used to determine causation between regime type and corruption.
choices.explanation: The CPI is a perception-based index, not a direct measurement of corruption incidents; it cannot establish causation and reflects analyst judgments, which introduces potential bias and limitations.

choices.text: As one comparative data point among many, while acknowledging its limitations as a perception-based constructed index.
choices.explanation: Per MPA-1.A.6 and MPA-1.A.8f, political scientists use indices like the CPI to compare countries and support inferences; but as a constructed perception measure, it should be treated as one tool among many, not as definitive proof of causation or actual corruption levels.

choices.text: Only as evidence about countries in the bottom quartile of the rankings, since wealthy countries have no corruption.
choices.explanation: Corruption perceptions exist across all country income levels; restricting CPI analysis to low-ranked countries would ignore the comparative insights available across the full distribution.

choices.text: As proof that democratic regimes are always less corrupt than authoritarian regimes.
choices.explanation: While democratic regimes on average score higher on the CPI, the relationship is not absolute; some democratic countries have significant corruption, and using the CPI to 'prove' an invariant causal claim confuses correlation with causation.

questions.subject: ap-comparative-government

stimulus.content: "Researchers studying the relationship between oil dependence and democratic governance find a consistent pattern: states that derive a high proportion of government revenue from oil exports tend to have weaker democratic institutions, higher corruption, and less governmental accountability to citizens. However, exceptions exist — some oil-exporting states have maintained democratic governance while others have become authoritarian without significant oil wealth."

questions.question: Which of the following methodological conclusions is most consistent with the findings described in the passage?

choices.text: Oil dependence and weak democracy are perfectly correlated, meaning one always and completely predicts the other.
choices.explanation: The passage explicitly notes 'exceptions exist,' meaning the relationship is not perfect; a perfect correlation would admit no exceptions.

choices.text: Oil dependence directly and invariably causes weak democratic institutions in all cases, as conclusively demonstrated by the consistent cross-national pattern the researchers identified in their data.
choices.explanation: Per MPA-1.A.3, causation is difficult to establish in comparative politics due to multiple confounding variables; identifying a pattern (correlation) does not establish invariant causation — the exceptions noted in the passage further complicate any causal claim.

choices.text: Oil dependence correlates with weaker democracy, but multiple confounders prevent a clean causal claim.
choices.explanation: MPA-1.A.3 and MPA-1.A.4 establish that comparative politics patterns reflect correlations, not confirmed causal chains; the passage's 'consistent pattern with exceptions' description is precisely the language of correlation, not causation.

choices.text: Because exceptions exist, the researchers' finding is completely invalid and should be discarded.
choices.explanation: Exceptions do not invalidate correlational findings; they indicate that the relationship is probabilistic rather than deterministic and that other variables also matter — this is normal for comparative political research, not a reason to discard findings.

questions.subject: ap-comparative-government

stimulus.content: "The United Kingdom's constitutional monarchy illustrates how traditional authority can coexist with rational-legal governance. The monarch is head of state by hereditary succession — a form of traditional legitimacy. Yet the monarch's political powers are exercised almost entirely through elected ministers acting under constitutional conventions, grounding actual governance in rational-legal authority."

questions.question: The passage best illustrates which of the following about sources of legitimacy?

choices.text: A state can draw on multiple distinct sources of political legitimacy simultaneously.
choices.explanation: Per LEG-1.A.2, sources of legitimacy include tradition, constitutional provisions, popular elections, and institutional effectiveness; the UK's combination of hereditary monarchy (tradition) with elected parliamentary governance (rational-legal) exemplifies how a single state can blend multiple legitimacy sources.

choices.text: Traditional authority is incompatible with democratic governance.
choices.explanation: The UK is a fully functioning liberal democracy with a traditional monarchy; the passage explicitly shows these two forms of authority coexisting — traditional authority and democratic legitimacy are not mutually exclusive.

choices.text: The UK's legitimacy derives exclusively from popular elections to the House of Commons.
choices.explanation: The passage specifically mentions the hereditary monarchy as a source of traditional legitimacy — reducing UK legitimacy to elections alone ignores this additional source acknowledged in the passage.

choices.text: Rational-legal authority always replaces traditional authority over time in democratic regimes.
choices.explanation: The UK maintains its hereditary monarchy alongside rational-legal governance; tradition has not been replaced but rather incorporated into a broader multi-source legitimacy framework.

questions.subject: ap-comparative-government

stimulus.content: "Mexico's gender quota legislation, implemented progressively since the early 2000s, requires that political parties nominate at least 50% female candidates for legislative elections. The Independent Electoral Institute (INE) enforces compliance. As a result, women's representation in Mexico's Chamber of Deputies has risen substantially, exceeding 50% by the early 2020s."

questions.question: The Mexican gender quota policy described in the passage best illustrates which of the following aspects of democratization?

choices.text: Democratic rule changes can accommodate ethnic diversity and increase multiparty competition.
choices.explanation: Gender quotas specifically address gender representation; while PAU-1.C.2 mentions both ethnic diversity and gender quotas, the passage is specifically about gender — characterizing it as an ethnic diversity measure misidentifies the policy's focus.

choices.text: Electoral rule changes such as gender quotas can advance democratization by broadening representation.
choices.explanation: Per PAU-1.C.2, democratic electoral systems can accommodate diversity with rule adjustments including gender quotas; PAU-1.C.1f (equal treatment of citizens) is directly advanced when women's political representation rises to reflect population parity.

choices.text: Gender quotas fundamentally undermine democratic legitimacy and popular sovereignty by mandating who must be nominated, thereby restricting the pool of candidates voters may freely choose among at the ballot box.
choices.explanation: The CED presents gender quotas as a democratization tool; they regulate who is nominated, not who wins — voters still choose among candidates; the CED does not frame quotas as undermining democratic legitimacy.

choices.text: Mexico's progress on gender representation proves that Mexico has achieved democratic consolidation.
choices.explanation: Legislative gender representation is one indicator of democratic progress, not proof of full consolidation; democratic consolidation requires broadly embedded institutions across multiple dimensions, not achievement on a single metric.

questions.subject: ap-comparative-government

stimulus.content: "Boko Haram, an Islamist insurgency operating primarily in northeastern Nigeria, has destabilized governance across multiple states. The group has attacked schools, kidnapped hundreds of students, and displaced millions of civilians. The Nigerian state has responded with military force, but has struggled to contain the insurgency, raising questions about the central government's capacity to project authority across its territory."

questions.question: The situation described in the passage is best analyzed using which of the following concepts?

choices.text: Democratic consolidation, as Nigeria demonstrates the maturity of its institutions through counterterrorism operations
choices.explanation: Struggling to contain an insurgency that has displaced millions does not demonstrate institutional maturity; the passage describes a regime stability challenge, not a successful demonstration of consolidation.

choices.text: A non-state internal actor threatening regime stability and undermining state sovereignty across its territory
choices.explanation: Per LEG-1.C.1b, Boko Haram is an explicitly named CED example of an internal actor threatening regime stability; the group's disruption of governance across multiple states challenges the Nigerian state's sovereignty and territorial control.

choices.text: Devolution of power from the central Nigerian government to the northeastern states
choices.explanation: The passage describes the federal government's struggle to project authority into the northeast — this is a loss of effective control, not a voluntary policy decision to devolve powers to subnational governments.

choices.text: A separatist movement seeking recognition of an independent northeastern Nigerian state
choices.explanation: Boko Haram's goal is establishing an Islamic state governed by its interpretation of Sharia law; while this implies territorial control ambitions, the CED characterizes it as an insurgency threatening stability, not a formal separatist movement seeking internationally recognized independence.

questions.subject: ap-comparative-government

stimulus.content: "Two scholars debate: Scholar A argues, 'Russia's democratization process has clearly backslid — the manipulated elections, restricted media, and suppressed opposition show that democratic gains after 1991 have been systematically reversed.' Scholar B responds, 'Russia never completed a democratic transition in the first place; it was always a hybrid regime that used democratic forms to legitimize authoritarian practices.'"

questions.question: Which of the following most accurately evaluates the debate between the two scholars?

choices.text: Scholar A is correct because Russia held relatively free multi-candidate elections in the early 1990s, demonstrating it had achieved a fully consolidated democracy before the systematic backsliding of the Putin era.
choices.explanation: Holding competitive elections is not the same as achieving consolidation; the 1990s elections were imperfect and institutions remained fragile — Russia's democratic transition was incomplete, making it contestable whether a fully consolidated democracy existed to backslide from.

choices.text: Scholar B is correct because Russia has always been classified as 'Not Free' by Freedom House since its independence.
choices.explanation: Russia was classified as 'Partly Free' by Freedom House in the 1990s before declining to 'Not Free' — this empirical fact contradicts Scholar B's claim that Russia was always authoritarian.

choices.text: Both scholars are partly right: Russia had genuine democratic openings after 1991 that were later systematically reversed.
choices.explanation: PAU-1.C.4 explicitly states that democratization can stall or be reversed; PAU-1.B.3 classifies hybrid/illiberal democracies as a distinct category; Russia's trajectory — brief democratic opening followed by managed democracy — is consistent with both scholars' partial insights and the CED framework.

choices.text: Neither scholar can be evaluated because comparative politics cannot distinguish between regime change and government change.
choices.explanation: Comparative politics explicitly distinguishes regime from government (PAU-1.A.2); the debate can be evaluated using these analytical tools — dismissing both scholars because of methodological impossibility is incorrect.

questions.subject: ap-comparative-government
questions.question: Which of the following best defines political corruption in the context of AP Comparative Government?

choices.text: Any policy decision made by a government that citizens disagree with
choices.explanation: Unpopular policy decisions are a normal part of democratic governance; political corruption specifically involves the abuse of entrusted power for private gain, not mere policy disagreement.

choices.text: The abuse of entrusted public power by government officials for private gain
choices.explanation: Per PAU-1.C.3, political corruption involves officials misusing their authority for personal benefit; it inhibits democratization and undermines the legitimacy of state institutions.

choices.text: The process by which authoritarian leaders use propaganda to maintain power
choices.explanation: While propaganda is a tool of authoritarian control, it is analytically distinct from corruption; corruption involves personal material gain from public office, not information control strategies.

choices.text: Any electoral outcome that does not reflect the population's preferred candidate
choices.explanation: Electoral fraud is a specific form of corruption, but political corruption is a broader concept encompassing bribery, embezzlement, nepotism, and other abuses of office beyond just electoral manipulation.

questions.subject: ap-comparative-government

stimulus.content: "In the 1990s, as Russia's economy collapsed and living standards fell sharply, the Yeltsin government's approval ratings plummeted. By the early 2000s, rising oil prices boosted government revenues and allowed for increased social spending and economic stabilization. President Putin's approval ratings rose substantially as citizens' material conditions improved."

questions.question: The pattern described in the passage best illustrates which of the following relationships?

choices.text: Reduced political corruption leads directly to improved citizen welfare.
choices.explanation: The passage describes economic conditions and approval ratings, not corruption levels; the mechanism discussed is economic performance affecting legitimacy, not corruption reduction.

choices.text: Economic performance shapes legitimacy: living standards rising or falling shift public approval.
choices.explanation: Per LEG-1.B.2 and LEG-1.B.3, economic development reinforces legitimacy while economic problems undermine it; the passage precisely illustrates this dynamic — poor performance under Yeltsin → legitimacy crisis; improved performance under Putin → higher approval.

choices.text: High oil prices cause democratic consolidation in oil-dependent states.
choices.explanation: The passage shows improved legitimacy, not democratic consolidation; Russia's managed democracy actually became more entrenched during the oil boom, not more consolidated democratically.

choices.text: Citizens living under authoritarian or hybrid regimes can never express dissatisfaction with their government's economic performance or policy choices.
choices.explanation: The passage shows that Russian citizens' declining approval of Yeltsin's government was reflected in approval ratings — dissatisfaction can be expressed and measured even in hybrid regimes.

questions.subject: ap-comparative-government

stimulus.content: "Country A holds free and fair elections, maintains an independent judiciary, protects civil liberties, and allows robust civil society activity. Country B holds elections but restricts opposition party registration, controls broadcast media through state ownership, uses courts to prosecute political rivals, and limits NGO activities through foreign-agent registration laws. Country C prohibits all competitive elections, operates a state ideology through mandatory education, and has no independent civil society."

questions.question: Which of the following accurately classifies Countries A, B, and C on the regime spectrum?

choices.text: A = liberal democracy; B = managed democracy or competitive authoritarian; C = one-party authoritarian state
choices.explanation: Country A meets all PAU-1.B.1 criteria for liberal democracy; Country B's combination of elections with systematic manipulation matches PAU-1.B.3's hybrid/illiberal democracy and PAU-1.D.1d's managed democracy; Country C's prohibition of competitive elections and mandatory state ideology matches a one-party authoritarian state.

choices.text: A = liberal democracy; B = theocracy based on religious law; C = managed democracy with restricted elections
choices.explanation: Country B's description (election manipulation through registration barriers, state media, court prosecution) matches managed democracy or competitive authoritarianism, not theocracy; Country C prohibits elections entirely, making it more authoritarian than managed democracy.

choices.text: A = totalitarian government that controls all aspects of citizens' lives; B = liberal democracy; C = hybrid regime
choices.explanation: Country A's attributes — free elections, independent judiciary, civil liberties — define liberal democracy, not totalitarianism; totalitarianism involves near-total state control of all aspects of life, which Country A clearly does not exhibit.

choices.text: All three are democracies because they all hold elections of some kind.
choices.explanation: The mere holding of elections does not establish democracy; Country B manipulates elections systematically, and Country C prohibits competitive ones — both are authoritarian or hybrid regimes despite electoral elements.

questions.subject: ap-comparative-government

stimulus.content: "An observer notes that citizens in some countries widely accept their government's authority and comply with its directives even in the absence of direct coercion, while in other countries governments must rely heavily on force and surveillance to maintain control. Political scientists use the concept of ____________ to describe the quality that explains citizen acceptance of government authority without direct force."

questions.question: Which term best fills the blank in the passage?

choices.text: Sovereignty
choices.explanation: Sovereignty describes a state's supreme legal authority over its territory, not citizens' psychological acceptance of that authority; a government can be sovereign without being considered legitimate by its people.

choices.text: Legitimacy
choices.explanation: Per LEG-1.A.1, legitimacy refers to whether citizens believe their government has the right to use power; when legitimacy is high, citizens comply voluntarily — when it is low, governments must resort more heavily to coercion.

choices.text: Regime
choices.explanation: A regime is the fundamental set of rules governing power; it is a structural concept, not the psychological quality describing citizen acceptance — that quality is legitimacy.

choices.text: Democratic consolidation
choices.explanation: Democratic consolidation refers to a developmental stage of democratic regimes; it is not the generic quality explaining citizen acceptance of government authority across all regime types.

questions.subject: ap-comparative-government

stimulus.content: "Political scientists argue about whether the devolution of power from central to regional governments in federal or quasi-federal arrangements strengthens or weakens regime legitimacy. Proponents argue that devolution improves policy outcomes by matching policies to local needs and increasing political participation. Critics argue that devolution creates contradictory policies, increases regional inequality, and can exacerbate ethnic tensions."

questions.question: Which course country best exemplifies the critics' concern that devolution can exacerbate ethnic tensions within a state?

choices.text: China, whose devolution of power to Tibet led to successful cultural autonomy
choices.explanation: China has not granted meaningful devolution to Tibet; it has suppressed Tibetan autonomy demands — the opposite of successful devolution; moreover, China is a unitary state that has not devolved power to minority regions in the way the question envisions.

choices.text: Nigeria, where northern states adopted Sharia courts under federalism, deepening religious cleavage
choices.explanation: Per LEG-1.B.4b, devolution can exacerbate ethnic and local tensions; Nigeria's federal system allowing northern states to implement Sharia courts has deepened the north-south religious cleavage — a prime example of federalism intensifying rather than resolving social divisions.

choices.text: The United Kingdom, which ended devolution to Scotland after it intensified independence demands
choices.explanation: The UK has not ended devolution; Scottish devolution is ongoing, and while it has been accompanied by growing independence sentiment, describing it as ended misrepresents the current situation.

choices.text: Mexico, whose federal system effectively shielded indigenous Zapatista communities in Chiapas from the regional economic inequality and land dispossession that otherwise affected surrounding communities
choices.explanation: Mexican federalism has not eliminated regional inequality — the Zapatista uprising arose precisely from severe inequality between indigenous communities and other Mexicans; federalism neither prevented this inequality nor resolved the Zapatista grievance.

questions.subject: ap-comparative-government

stimulus.content: "Iran's Guardian Council reviews all candidates for the presidency and Majles to ensure they meet Islamic qualifications and support the velayat-e faqih doctrine. In 2021, it disqualified many reformist and moderate candidates from the presidential race, leaving only conservative candidates approved to participate."

questions.question: The Guardian Council's role as described in the passage most directly illustrates which concept?

choices.text: Democratic consolidation through independent electoral management
choices.explanation: Democratic consolidation requires genuinely independent institutions; the Guardian Council is appointed by the Supreme Leader and restricts competition — the opposite of an independent electoral management body supporting consolidation.

choices.text: Managed democracy, where the regime vets candidates to ensure only acceptable outcomes
choices.explanation: The Guardian Council's candidate vetting is a mechanism by which Iran's regime controls who can participate in elections, ensuring outcomes acceptable to the clerical establishment — a form of managed competition that limits genuine electoral choice.

choices.text: A proportional representation system designed to increase minority political participation in the legislature
choices.explanation: The passage describes candidate disqualification, not the vote-counting method; proportional representation relates to how votes translate into seats, not to who is permitted to run.

choices.text: Independent civil society organizations monitoring and reporting on government malfeasance
choices.explanation: The Guardian Council is a state body appointed by the Supreme Leader, not an independent civil society organization; it enforces clerical control over elections rather than monitoring the government on behalf of citizens.

questions.subject: ap-comparative-government

stimulus.content: "The Human Development Index (HDI) combines three dimensions: life expectancy at birth, mean years of schooling and expected years of schooling, and gross national income per capita. It is designed to capture aspects of human well-being beyond income alone."

questions.question: Which of the following best explains why political scientists use the HDI alongside GDP per capita rather than relying on GDP per capita alone?

choices.text: GDP per capita is a normative measure, while HDI is an empirical measure.
choices.explanation: Both GDP per capita and HDI are empirical measures derived from observable data; neither is normative — the distinction is about what dimensions of development each captures, not whether they are factual or value-based.

choices.text: HDI captures health and education that income alone misses, giving a fuller picture of human development.
